title: Why you fall sick at season-change
excerpt: Every time the season shifts, your body needs a two-week bridge to adjust. Ayurveda calls this window ritu sandhi, and knowing it can stop the familiar cold, rash, or fatigue before it starts.
at_a_glance: Ritu sandhi is the 14-day junction between seasons, 7 days from each side, when the body is managing two conflicting seasonal influences at once.
Adopt the incoming season's diet and habits gradually over the first week rather than switching all at once, to protect digestive strength.
Warm, freshly cooked meals, consistent meal times, and a brief warm oil self-massage are the classical tools for crossing the junction without falling ill.

body.heading: that familiar sniffle at season-change
paragraphs: You have probably noticed it: the rains arrive or the summer heat fades, and within a week or two you are down with a cold, a skin flare-up, or a tiredness you cannot explain. Your colleagues have it too, your children have it, and everyone shrugs and says "it is the weather." Ayurveda agrees, but it goes further. It names this window, explains why the body becomes vulnerable, and gives you a practical way to cross it.

The transition period between two seasons is called ritu sandhi, which translates simply as the junction of seasons. Ritu means season, sandhi means joint or meeting point. Classical texts describe it as the final seven days of the departing season plus the first seven days of the arriving one: a fourteen-day bridge during which both seasonal qualities are present at the same time (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). During this fortnight, the qualities that the previous season left behind in your body, whether excess heat from summer or accumulated cold from winter, are still clearing out while the new season's qualities begin to accumulate. The body is managing two opposing influences at once, and that is precisely when it slips.

body.heading: what the granthas say about why we fall ill
paragraphs: Caraka Samhita explains that the human body stays healthy when its internal rhythms match the rhythms of the natural world (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). When a season changes abruptly, the doshas that were in equilibrium during the previous season are disrupted before the body has adapted to the new environment. Think of it as a lag, the way jet-lag throws off your sleep when your body clock does not yet match the local time.

Ashtanga Hridaya describes the recommended conduct for each season, and specifically notes that the rules of the incoming season should be adopted gradually, not all at once (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). This gradual adoption is the practical heart of the ritu sandhi principle. Eating heavy winter foods from day one of the cold season, or switching immediately to raw salads the moment spring arrives, is asking the digestive fire to change gears too fast. The texts connect this abrupt shift to diminished agni, the digestive capacity, which in turn allows ama (undigested residue) to build up and create the conditions for illness.

Bhavaprakasha reinforces this by listing the seasonal causes of common ailments, observing that viruses, skin conditions, and respiratory trouble tend to cluster at these junctions (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda). The insight is not mystical: it maps well onto what immunologists note about the stress of thermal and humidity shifts on the respiratory mucosa and immune regulation.

body.heading: a gentle two-week protocol for the junction
paragraphs: The classical advice is simple: in the last seven days of the outgoing season, begin reducing the foods and habits specific to that season. In the first seven days of the incoming season, adopt the new season's regimen slowly, not all at once. If you have been eating heavy, oily, warming foods through winter, start lightening your meals in the final week of winter before spring fully arrives. If you have been enjoying cooling, light foods in summer, do not immediately shift to heavy soups the moment the monsoon clouds appear.

Practically, this means favoring freshly cooked, warm, easily digestible meals during the junction: soft rice, moong dal, soups, and lightly spiced vegetables. Reduce raw foods, very cold drinks, and anything fermented or stale during this window. Keep meal times consistent and avoid skipping meals, since irregular eating weakens agni at the very time it needs steadiness.

Rest is part of the protocol too. The junction period is not the time to push through sleeplessness or skip your morning routine. Warm oil self-massage (abhyanga) once or twice during the week before and after the seasonal shift is mentioned in the classical literature as a way to keep the skin barrier strong and the nervous system calm (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Even a five-minute warm sesame oil rub before a bath counts.

Simple kitchen spices help maintain digestive strength during this window. Ginger, cumin, and coriander are all considered digestive aids in the classical texts, appropriate for most people in moderate amounts. A warm cumin-coriander-fennel tea, prepared by steeping half a teaspoon of each in hot water for five minutes, is a traditional household remedy for supporting agni at season junctions.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: The ritu sandhi principle is useful for staying well, but it is not a substitute for medical care when symptoms become serious. See a qualified doctor or vaidya if you develop a high fever, severe breathlessness, a rash that is spreading rapidly, chest pain, persistent vomiting or diarrhea, or any symptom that is worsening over more than a few days despite rest and simple care. Children, the elderly, and anyone with chronic conditions such as asthma, diabetes, or a compromised immune system should be especially prompt about seeking care at season-change, since they are more vulnerable to complications.

This article is general health information grounded in classical Ayurvedic texts. It is not a diagnosis, and it is not a replacement for an evaluation by a licensed medical professional or trained vaidya who can assess your individual constitution and current state of health.

try_this_today.title: Wind down the old season's diet
try_this_today.description: In the final seven days before a season changes, start reducing foods characteristic of that season. Lighter, simpler meals ease the transition and keep agni stable.

try_this_today.title: Ease into the new season's foods
try_this_today.description: In the first week of the new season, introduce seasonal foods one at a time rather than overhauling your diet overnight. Your digestive fire needs time to recalibrate.

try_this_today.title: Cumin-coriander-fennel tea
try_this_today.description: Steep half a teaspoon each of cumin, coriander, and fennel seeds in a cup of just-boiled water for five minutes. Strain and sip warm, once or twice a day during the junction fortnight.

try_this_today.title: Warm oil self-massage once a week
try_this_today.description: Before your bath, rub warm sesame oil over your limbs and torso for five minutes. This supports the skin barrier and calms the nervous system during the transition window.

try_this_today.title: Keep meal times steady
try_this_today.description: Irregular eating weakens agni at the worst possible time. Aim for meals at consistent times during the junction, and avoid skipping lunch, the meal tied to the strongest digestive window.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: Kindles digestive fire and reduces bloating, useful for maintaining agni during the season-junction fortnight.

ingredients.name: Coriander
ingredients.description: Cooling and carminative, balances excess heat lingering from the outgoing season while supporting gentle digestion.

ingredients.name: Fennel
ingredients.description: Relieves digestive discomfort and supports the gradual shift in digestive patterns as the new season's foods are introduced.

ingredients.name: Ginger
ingredients.description: A classical deepana herb that strengthens agni; a small slice of fresh ginger with rock salt before meals is a traditional junction-season practice.

words_to_know.definition: The junction between two seasons: the final seven days of the departing season plus the first seven days of the arriving one.
Season. Ayurveda recognises six seasons in a year, each associated with particular qualities and dietary rules.
Digestive fire or metabolic capacity. Strong agni converts food cleanly; weakened agni allows ama to accumulate.
Undigested or partially processed residue that builds up when agni is low, considered a root cause of many diseases.
Warm oil self-massage, a classical daily practice for nourishing the skin, calming the nervous system, and supporting immunity.
The three governing forces of the body (Vata, Pitta, Kapha) whose balance determines health; each is influenced by seasonal qualities.

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana (seasonal regimen and ritu sandhi conduct) · Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana (dinacharya and ritucharya, alignment of individual rhythms with natural cycles) · Bhavaprakasha, seasonal regimen and disease predisposition at ritu sandhi
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
